# Smart material

Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *Find sources:* ["Smart material"](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22Smart+material%22) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22Smart+material%22+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Smart+material%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Smart+material%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Smart+material%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Smart+material%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(November 2013)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/9/99/Question_book-new.svg/60px-Question_book-new.svg.png)

**Smart materials**, also called intelligent or responsive materials,[[1]](#cite_note-1)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] are designed materials that have one or more properties that can be significantly changed in a controlled fashion by external stimuli, such as [stress](/wiki/Stress_(physics) "Stress (physics)"), moisture, [electric](/wiki/Electric_field "Electric field") or [magnetic](/wiki/Magnetic_field "Magnetic field") fields, light, [temperature](/wiki/Temperature "Temperature"), [pH](/wiki/PH "PH"), or chemical compounds.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) Smart materials are the basis of many applications, including [sensors](/wiki/Sensors "Sensors") and [actuators](/wiki/Actuators "Actuators"), or [artificial muscles](/wiki/Artificial_muscles "Artificial muscles"), particularly as [electroactive polymers](/wiki/Electroactive_polymers "Electroactive polymers") (EAPs).[[4]](#cite_note-4)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*][[5]](#cite_note-5)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*][[6]](#cite_note-6)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*][[7]](#cite_note-7)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*][[8]](#cite_note-8)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*][[9]](#cite_note-9)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

# What are smart materials?

![Hero Image](https://images.prismic.io/greenly/Z9rk8TiBA97Giqd5_Templateimage-1-.png?w=896&h=256&fit=max&dpr=3)
![Kara Anderson](https://images.prismic.io/greenly/f9859071-2178-4b79-8ed7-3269e73cdd6e_Image-1+%281%29.jpg?fit=max&w=3000&dpr=3)
![Hero Image](https://images.prismic.io/greenly/Z9rk8TiBA97Giqd5_Templateimage-1-.png?w=896&h=256&fit=max&dpr=3)
![construction site](https://images.prismic.io/greenly/Z9rk8TiBA97Giqd5_Templateimage-1-.png?w=896&h=256&fit=max&dpr=3)

The construction industry is one of the biggest contributors to global carbon [emissions](https://greenly.earth/en-us/blog/ecology-news/carbon-emissions-what-you-need-to-know), responsible for around [37%](https://www.weforum.org/stories/2024/06/building-sector-climate-change-construction-materials/#:~:text=The%20building%20value%20chain%20is,green%20transition%20from%20now%20on.) of total emissions. With the urgency of [climate change](https://greenly.earth/en-us/blog/ecology-news/climate-change-in-2022-where-do-we-stand), there’s growing pressure to find ways to build more sustainably: reducing waste, improving energy efficiency, and extending the lifespan of buildings.

One of the most interesting developments in this space is the rise of smart materials - ie. building materials designed to adapt to their environment in real time. **Unlike traditional materials, which remain unchanged once installed, smart materials can react to heat, moisture, or light, helping to reduce energy use and cut down on maintenance.** Self-healing concrete, for example, can repair its own cracks, while thermochromic glass tints automatically to keep buildings cool.

**In this article, we’ll explore what smart materials are, how they work, and why they’re key to building a more sustainable future.**

## What are smart materials?

**These materials operate on the principle of self-adaptation, meaning they can actively improve building efficiency and durability without human interference.** Some smart materials are capable of self-repairing, while others can regulate temperature or energy use to optimize performance. This makes them especially valuable in sustainable construction, where reducing waste and energy consumption is a priority.

The key characteristics that define smart materials include:

| Characteristic | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Responsiveness | Reacts to environmental changes such as heat, moisture, pressure, or light. |
| Self-Regulation | Adjusts properties automatically to improve efficiency and performance. |
| Durability | Extends the lifespan of buildings by resisting wear and tear or self-healing damage. |
| Energy Efficiency | Helps regulate temperature, reduce energy demand, and lower emissions. |
| Sustainability | Reduces the need for maintenance and repairs, leading to less material waste. |

![youtube screenshot](https://images.prismic.io/greenly/Z9rmUTiBA97Giqfr_youtube.jpg?w=894&fit=max&dpr=3)

## Types of smart materials used in construction

[Smart materials](https://www.mapfreglobalrisks.com/en/risks-insurance-management/article/smart-materials-building-future/) are already being integrated into construction projects to create more [energy-efficient](https://greenly.earth/en-us/blog/company-guide/all-you-need-to-know-about-energy-efficiency), durable, and low-maintenance buildings. Below are some of the most promising types of smart materials helping to transform the industry.

### Self-healing concrete

Concrete is the most widely used building material, but it is prone to cracking due to stress, temperature changes, and environmental exposure. These cracks weaken structures over time and can allow water to seep in, leading to expensive repairs and even potential structural failure.

**Producing cement - the key ingredient in [concrete](https://greenly.earth/en-us/blog/industries/what-is-the-carbon-footprint-of-concrete) - is highly carbon-intensive, accounting for nearly [8%](https://www.weforum.org/stories/2024/09/cement-production-sustainable-concrete-co2-emissions/) of global CO₂ emissions.** This makes reducing the need for repairs and replacements crucial for sustainability in construction.

**Self-healing [concrete](https://gccassociation.org/essential-concrete/self-healing-concrete/) helps solve this problem by automatically repairing cracks, reducing maintenance, and extending the lifespan of buildings and infrastructure.**

#### How it works:

Self-healing concrete contains special additives that react to environmental triggers, such as water or air, to seal cracks. The most common methods include:

#### Uses in construction:

| Application | How It Works | Benefit |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Bridges and Roads | Bacteria or capsules seal cracks caused by heavy loads and weather. | Extends infrastructure lifespan and reduces repair costs. |
| Tunnels and Foundations | Self-healing prevents water infiltration. | Improves durability and prevents leaks. |
| High-Rise Buildings | Embedded agents repair microcracks. | Enhances structural safety and reduces maintenance. |

### Thermochromic and photochromic materials

Buildings often rely on artificial lighting and climate control systems ([AC](https://greenly.earth/en-us/blog/industries/air-conditioner-is-bad-for-the-environment--but-is-there-a-choice)) to maintain comfortable indoor conditions, which can result in high energy consumption and carbon emissions. **Thermochromic and photochromic materials provide a passive, energy-efficient alternative by automatically adjusting to environmental changes, and reducing the need for air conditioning, heating, and artificial lighting.**

#### How they work:

#### Uses in construction:

| Application | How It Works | Benefit |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Thermochromic Glass (Smart Windows) | Glass embedded with thermochromic coatings darkens as temperatures rise, blocking excess solar heat. | Reduces cooling costs in summer and prevents overheating in buildings. |
| Photochromic Glass | Glass automatically darkens in response to strong UV exposure and lightens in dim conditions. | Regulates brightness indoors, reducing glare and the need for artificial lighting. |
| Thermochromic Paints and Coatings | Paints contain pigments that change color based on temperature, reflecting or absorbing heat accordingly. | Helps to maintain stable indoor temperatures by adapting to seasonal variations. |
| Dynamic Roofing Materials | Roofing membranes coated with thermochromic layers adjust reflectivity based on ambient heat levels. | Enhances insulation and reduces energy costs for heating and cooling. |

### Shape memory alloys and polymers

**[Shape memory](https://www.azobuild.com/article.aspx?ArticleID=8386) alloys (SMAs) and shape memory polymers (SMPs) are materials that can ‘remember’ and return to a predetermined shape when exposed to a specific trigger, such as heat, electricity, or mechanical stress.** This ability makes them incredibly useful in construction, where materials often face stress, temperature fluctuations, and environmental wear over time.

#### How they work:

#### Uses in construction:

| Application | How It Works | Benefit |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Self-Repairing Structural Components | SMAs embedded in buildings and bridges correct deformations caused by stress or earthquakes when heated. | Extends the lifespan of infrastructure and reduces maintenance costs. |
| Adaptive Façades and Ventilation Systems | SMP-based roofing panels, window shades, or vents expand or contract in response to temperature changes. | Improves energy efficiency by reducing heating and cooling demands. |
| Seismic-Resistant Structures | SMA reinforcements absorb shock and return to their original form after seismic activity. | Enhances building resilience in earthquake-prone areas. |
| Smart Fasteners and Joints | SMAs in connectors tighten or loosen based on temperature shifts. | Increases flexibility in modular construction and reduces structural stress. |

### Phase-change materials (PCMs) for temperature control

Managing indoor temperatures in buildings typically requires high energy consumption for heating and cooling, contributing to carbon emissions and high operational costs. **Phase-change materials ([PCMs](https://thermtest.com/phase-change-material-pcm)) offer a passive, energy-efficient solution by absorbing, storing, and releasing heat as they transition between solid and liquid states.**

#### How they work:

This thermal buffering effect reduces temperature fluctuations inside buildings, minimizing the need for heating in winter and air conditioning in summer.

#### Uses in construction:

| Application | How It Works | Benefit |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Wall and Ceiling Panels | PCMs integrated into construction materials absorb and release heat as needed. | Enhances indoor comfort and reduces HVAC energy use. |
| Roofing Systems | PCM layers regulate temperature by preventing excessive heat gain or loss. | Lowers cooling and heating costs. |
| Flooring and Underfloor Heating | PCMs store heat from sunlight or radiant heating and release it gradually. | Improves energy efficiency and reduces peak energy demand. |

### Aerogels – ultra-light insulation

Effective insulation is essential for reducing heat loss in winter and preventing overheating in summer, but many traditional insulation materials are bulky, heavy, or inefficient over time. **[Aerogels](https://www.azobuild.com/article.aspx?ArticleID=8656) provide an innovative alternative, offering ultra-lightweight, high-performance insulation with minimal material use.**

#### How they work:

Aerogels are composed of up to 99 percent air, with a highly porous structure that traps heat and prevents thermal transfer. Despite their incredibly low density, they have some of the best insulation properties of any known material, outperforming fiberglass and foam insulation.

#### Uses in construction:

| Application | How It Works | Benefit |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Wall and Roof Insulation | Aerogel panels or coatings minimize heat loss while remaining ultra-thin. | Reduces heating and cooling costs without adding bulk. |
| Window Coatings | Transparent aerogels improve insulation without blocking natural light. | Enhances energy efficiency while maintaining daylight access. |
| Pipe and HVAC Duct Insulation | Aerogel wraps prevent heat loss in pipes and ventilation systems. | Improves heating system efficiency and reduces energy waste. |

### Electrochromic glass (smart windows)

Windows are one of the biggest sources of heat loss in winter and heat gain in summer, forcing buildings to rely on heating, air conditioning, and artificial lighting to maintain comfortable indoor conditions. **[Electrochromic](https://www.smartglassworld.net/electrochromic-glass) glass offers an energy-efficient solution by automatically adjusting its tint in response to electrical signals.**

Unlike traditional windows, which require curtains, blinds, or reflective films to provide shade, electrochromic glass can switch between clear and tinted states on demand, helping buildings optimize light and temperature without obstructing views or blocking natural daylight.

#### How it works:

#### Use in construction:

| Application | How It Works | Benefit |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Smart Windows | Glass darkens or clears based on electrical signals. | Reduces glare, heat gain, and cooling energy demand. |
| Skylights and Glass Roofs | Electrochromic technology manages natural light exposure. | Maintains comfortable indoor temperatures while reducing artificial lighting use. |
| Glass Façades and High-Rise Buildings | Adaptive glass regulates light and heat transmission. | Improves energy efficiency and enhances occupant comfort. |

![youtube screenshot](https://images.prismic.io/greenly/Z9ro_jiBA97Giqjh_youtube.jpg?w=894&fit=max&dpr=3)

## Why are smart materials important for sustainable construction?

**The construction sector faces three major sustainability challenges: high energy consumption, material waste, and environmental degradation.** Traditional buildings require constant heating and cooling, rely on finite raw materials, and often need frequent repairs and renovations, all of which contribute to carbon emissions and resource depletion.

**Smart materials offer a way to build more efficiently by making structures adaptable, durable, and less dependent on external energy inputs.** Instead of relying solely on mechanical systems to regulate indoor temperatures or maintain structural integrity, these materials can respond dynamically to environmental conditions, improving a building’s overall sustainability.

**How smart materials contribute to sustainable construction:**

| Challenge | How Smart Materials Help |
| --- | --- |
| Excessive Energy Use in Buildings | Electrochromic glass and phase-change materials regulate heat and light, reducing the need for air conditioning and artificial lighting. |
| High Carbon Footprint of Cement and Concrete | Self-healing concrete extends the life of structures, reducing the need for new concrete production—a major source of CO₂ emissions. |
| Material Waste and Short Lifespans | Shape-memory alloys and self-repairing materials help buildings withstand wear and tear, minimizing the need for replacements. |
| Lack of Passive Adaptation to Climate | Thermochromic and photochromic materials allow buildings to adjust naturally to temperature and light fluctuations. |
| Vulnerability to Extreme Weather | Advanced insulation materials like aerogels improve resilience against extreme heat and cold, making buildings more climate-adaptive. |

**Long-term benefits of smart materials:**

## Challenges and limitations of smart materials

While smart materials offer significant environmental and economic benefits, their widespread adoption in construction is still limited by several challenges. **High costs, production complexities, and integration issues make it difficult for the industry to transition away from conventional materials at scale.**

### High initial costs

Many smart materials, such as electrochromic glass, aerogels, and self-healing concrete, require specialized production processes that drive up costs. **Compared to traditional materials, they can be significantly more expensive, making upfront investment a barrier.** However, as demand grows and manufacturing scales up, costs are expected to decline, making these technologies more accessible.

### Integration with existing infrastructure

Most buildings today are designed for static materials, meaning integrating adaptive materials can require structural or design modifications. **Adoption is much easier in new construction, but for older infrastructure, integration can be difficult.**

### Limited awareness and industry adoption

Many architects, developers, and contractors still rely on conventional building materials and practices due to familiarity, cost-effectiveness, and established supply chains. **Lack of awareness and technical expertise in using smart materials prevents wider adoption.** Government incentives and updated building codes that prioritize sustainable materials could help accelerate industry transition.

### Durability and long-term performance uncertainty

Some smart materials, such as phase-change materials and self-repairing composites, are still relatively new, and data on long-term durability is limited. While lab tests show promising results, real-world applications over decades will determine whether these materials can consistently perform under varying conditions. **Developers and engineers may need greater confidence in reliability before investing in large-scale projects.**

## The future of smart materials

**Ongoing research, technological advancements, and policy shifts are driving the development of smart materials.** As manufacturing costs decrease and new innovations emerge, smart materials are expected to become a standard feature of sustainable architecture.

### Advances in material science and nanotechnology

**Scientists are exploring ways to enhance the efficiency, durability, and affordability of smart materials using nanotechnology and bioengineering**. Some of the most promising developments include:

### What’s next for smart materials?

The next decade is likely to see:

**As technological advancements continue and costs decline, smart materials will play a central role in creating the energy-efficient, climate-resilient buildings of the future.**

## The future of clean energy

The clean energy sector is evolving rapidly, driven by **technological advancements, declining costs, and growing global demand for sustainable solutions**. While challenges remain, the long-term trajectory for renewables, energy storage, and emerging technologies is promising. Here’s what to expect in the coming years.

### The acceleration of solar and wind

Solar and wind are now the **cheapest sources of new electricity** in most countries, and their deployment is expected to continue at record levels.

### The rise of energy storage and grid modernization

Energy storage is crucial for addressing the intermittency of renewables, and **breakthroughs in battery technology** are making it more viable.

### Green hydrogen and the decarbonization of heavy industry

[Hydrogen](https://greenly.earth/en-us/blog/company-guide/what-is-gold-hydrogen) is emerging as a key **clean fuel** for sectors that are difficult to electrify, such as **steelmaking, shipping, and aviation**.

### Expansion of nuclear and emerging technologies

While controversial, **nuclear power is regaining momentum** as a reliable low-carbon energy source.

Other **emerging technologies** include:

### Policy and investment trends

Government policies and financial markets will play a crucial role in **determining the pace of the clean energy transition**.

## How Greenly can help your company

At Greenly, we help businesses track, analyze, and reduce their carbon footprint, making it easier to transition to more sustainable practices. While smart materials play a crucial role in building a low-carbon future, they are just one example of how innovation can drive sustainability. **To make a real impact, companies need comprehensive strategies to measure and reduce emissions across all operations**.

### How Greenly supports businesses in their sustainability journey

Greenly provides the expertise and technology to help businesses achieve measurable progress toward their sustainability goals. **Want to learn how Greenly can help your company cut emissions and accelerate its sustainability strategy?** [Get in touch with us today.](https://greenly.earth/en-us)

--- SOURCE 5: Smart Materials - Mide Technology ---
URL: https://www.mide.com/smart-materials

SUMMARY:
Smart Materials are materials that respond to changes in their environment and then undergo a material property change.

FULL CONTENT:
![Mide | Hutchinson](https://www.mide.com/hubfs/Logos/hutchinson-mide-color-white-web.svg "Mide Technology")

# Smart Materials

## **Smart Material Engineering Services**

The engineers of Midé possess a unique, broad level of expertise when it comes to the application of smart materials.  Smart Materials are materials that respond to changes in their environment and then undergo a material property change.  These property changes can be leveraged to create an actuator or a sensor from the materials without any additional control or electronics required.  Midé engineers understand the advantages and limitations of each material so the appropriate solution can be suggested for each of our customer’s applications.  Midé has the experience necessary to source materials, design and fabricate prototypes, perform modeling and simulation, and manufacture products.  The options of smart materials have a wide range as evidenced by the list below our sensor kit products.

Smart Material products

Sorry, there are no products matching your search.

## Types of Smart Materials

### Piezoelectrics:

Piezoelectric materials convert electrical energy to mechanical energy, and vice versa. They offer a wide range of utility and can be used as actuators (provide a voltage to create motion), sensors, such as many accelerometers, and energy harvesters since the charge generated from motion can be harvested and stored. Common applications for piezo materials are BBQ igniters and actuators for inkjet printer heads. Midé has successfully commercialized energy harvesters, haptic actuators, piezo valve actuators, and flow control devices.

### Shape Memory Alloys:

The most commonly available Shape Memory Alloy is Nitinol, which was originally developed by the Naval Ordinance Laboratory. SMA’s have the ability to change phase as a function of temperature, and in that process generate a force or motion. They are capable of relatively high energy but move slowly. Typically applications include morphing structures, thermal triggers, and some high strain energy absorbing applications. Advanced materials still under development include magnetically activated shape memory alloys.

### Magnetostrictive:

Similar to piezoelectric materials that respond to changes in electrical fields, this class of materials responds to changes in magnetic fields and can perform as an actuator, or sensor if deformed. While they can work well, they exhibit a large hysteresis which must be compensated when using the material in sensor applications.

### Shape Memory Polymers:

Shape Memory Polymers (SMP) are similar to Shape Memory Alloys except the obvious fact they are made from a polymer matrix. They possess much greater recoverable strains than the alloys, but typically under lower forces. Morphing structures has been the area of greatest use to date for SMP’s.

### Hydrogels:

Hydrogels can be tailored to absorb and hold water, or other liquids, under certain environmental conditions. Hydrogels have been around for a long time, specifically in disposable diapers. A key feature however is the gels can be tailored chemically to respond to different stimuli. Midé has also patented a method to embed the gels into a foam which enables systems to be built with the gels, such as the Hydrogel Activated Bulkhead Shaft Seals.

### Electroactive Polymers:

There are many forms of electroactive polymers and many are still being refined. They have great potential as the flexibility of how they can be used provide advantages over some of the metals and ceramics mentioned above. Most typically applications include energy harvesting and sensing (see Stretchsense development kit) however some researchers are looking at high voltage, low current actuators.

### Bi-Component Fibers:

Adaptive thermal insulation can enable smart clothing that can change its thermal properties based on the environment. Midé has developed bi-component fiber technology where two different materials are co-extruded together to enable shape change depending on ambient temperature.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

enriched_points.expanded_content: Smart materials are engineered to change properties in response to external stimuli, including stress, temperature, and moisture, enhancing functionality and adaptability in various applications.
They respond to multiple stimuli such as thermal, electrical, magnetic fields, and light, allowing them to serve diverse roles, including sensors, actuators, and artificial muscles.
Smart materials find applications in construction, automotive, aerospace, and medical sectors, improving energy efficiency, structural integrity, and functionality in innovative technologies.
